title: Writing: Schedules and Ages
learningGoal: Construct sentences stating your age and identifying the time and day.
instructions: In this exercise, you will practice writing sentences about time and age. Pay very close attention to the gender marker when writing your age (ka for men, ki for women).
writingPrompt:
Translate the following schedule into Hindi:
1. Today is Wednesday.
2. I am [use 'das' for 10] years old. (Assume you are a male for this test).
3. It is 4 o'clock.
4. Tomorrow is Thursday.
promptGoal: Building sentences using numbers, 'baje', and days of the week.
referenceHints: Sentence 3: 'Four o'clock are.' (Remember time uses plural 'hain')

modelExampleOutputs.targetText: आज बुधवार है। मैं दस साल का हूँ। चार बजे हैं। कल गुरुवार है।
modelExampleOutputs.transliteration: Aaj budhvaar hai. Main das saal ka hoon. Chaar baje hain. Kal guruvaar hai.
modelExampleOutputs.baseTranslation: Today is Wednesday. I am ten years old. It is four o'clock. Tomorrow is Thursday.
modelExampleOutputs.annotation: Perfect SOV order. Correct masculine gender marker 'ka'. Correct plural verb 'hain' for time.

evaluationCriteria.description: Correctly maps Aaj to Wednesday and Kal to Thursday.
Correctly forms 'Main das saal ka hoon'.

adminCorrectAnswerSet.sampleAnswer: आज बुधवार है। मैं दस साल का हूँ। चार बजे हैं। कल गुरुवार है।
acceptableAlternates: Aaj budhvaar hai. Main das saal ka hoon. Chaar baje hain. Kal guruvaar hai.
Aaj Budhvaar hai. Main das saal ki hoon. Chaar baje hain. Kal Guruvaar hai.
adminCorrectAnswerSet.grammarBaseline: Accepts transliteration. Checking verb endings closely.

criteria.name: Plurality of Time
criteria.description: Must use 'hain' for time (baje hain) because hours are countable plurals.

feedbackRules.onPass: Flawless! You can write a basic calendar and state your age.
feedbackRules.onFail: Please review how to tell time. You must use 'hain' (are), not 'hai' (is) when saying 'baje'.
feedbackRules.onPartial: Good try. Don't forget that 'Main' always ends with 'hoon', and time always ends with 'hain'.

hintText: Read the 'Reference Hints' for the exact literal word order in Hindi.
commonMistakes: Writing 'Chaar baje hai' instead of 'Chaar baje hain'.
